title: TCU vs Ohio State — First Round Viewing Guide
eventTag: NCAA Tournament • First Round • East Region
venue: Bon Secours Wellness Arena, Greenville, SC • CBS
extraInfo: Winner likely faces #1 overall seed Duke on Saturday

stakes.callout: An 8-9 matchup between two power-conference teams that caught fire late in the season. TCU won 9 of its last 10, including three wins over top-10 opponents. Ohio State's all-time leading scorer has never played in an NCAA Tournament game. Both teams rebuilt through the transfer portal. Both are playing with house money. And the winner likely draws #1 overall seed Duke on Saturday.

cards.label: For TCU
cards.value: Jamie Dixon came home to his alma mater to build something. After a 16-16 season last year, he gutted the roster and rebuilt. Now he's got a program-record 11 Big 12 wins and a team peaking at exactly the right time.

cards.label: For Ohio State
cards.value: Bruce Thornton is the all-time leading scorer in Ohio State history — and he's never played in a single NCAA Tournament game. This is his one shot. Jake Diebler's first tournament as a head coach. A three-year drought is over.

cards.label: The Momentum Factor
cards.value: TCU won 9 of 10 down the stretch, including upsets of #5 Iowa State, #10 Texas Tech, and #10 Florida. Ohio State beat #8 Purdue and pushed #1 Michigan to the wire in the Big Ten Tournament. Both teams enter March playing their best ball.

cards.label: The Prize
cards.value: The winner almost certainly faces #1 overall seed Duke (32-2) on Saturday in Greenville. That's the carrot — and the challenge — of this bracket pod.

styles.title: Interior Muscle That Runs
bullets: Tempo: Jamie Dixon's teams push the pace — TCU has led the country in fastbreak points in recent seasons. Dixon: "Everybody talks about running, nobody in the country has done it like we have."

Interior dominance: The Edmonds-Punch frontcourt is the engine. In the key upset at #10 Texas Tech, TCU outscored Tech 38-22 in the paint and out-rebounded them 39-25. When they control the boards, they're a different team.

Defensive disruption: 4.5 blocks per game (37th nationally) and 13.8 forced turnovers per game (35th). They run a "no middle" scheme that funnels the ball to the perimeter and punishes interior drives.
Clutch gene: Erased an 18-point deficit vs. K-State. Closed on a 12-0 run to beat #5 Iowa State. This team doesn't panic.

Weakness: Outside shooting is inconsistent (Punch .256 from three, Harding .271) and free throw shooting is a real liability (Punch .648, Harding .646). If they can't score inside, the offense stalls.

styles.title: The Bruce Thornton Show
bullets: Elite offense: KenPom's 16th-best offense nationally (118.6 rating). They shoot 58.7% on twos (top-20) and 77.5% from the line (20th). Low turnover rate. This team doesn't beat itself.

Guard-driven scoring: Four players average double figures, but Thornton is the headliner — 20.2 PPG on .558 FG% and .400 from three. He plays 90% of minutes. When he's on, Ohio State is genuinely dangerous.

The Mobley factor: John Mobley Jr. (.418 from three) returned from hand surgery with his fingers taped together and immediately dropped 21 on Purdue. When he's healthy and shooting, Ohio State has a lethal 1-2 backcourt punch.
Freshman wildcard: A'mare Bynum stepped in after Brandon Noel's injury and averaged 12.4 PPG in March. A 6-8, 240-pound athlete playing his best ball at the right time.
Weakness: Defense is the gap — 216th nationally in defensive rating. They're 4-11 in Quad 1 games overall. The offense can carry them, but against elite opponents, the defense has been exposed.

keys.title: TCU's interior vs. Ohio State's perimeter.
keys.description: This is the fundamental clash. TCU wants to pound it inside through Edmonds (.572 FG%) and Punch (14.3 PPG, 1.9 BPG). Ohio State wants to play through its guards. If TCU dominates the paint and boards like they did at Texas Tech, they win. If Ohio State forces TCU to shoot from outside — where Punch hits just .256 from three and Harding .271 — the Buckeyes take control.

keys.title: Free throws could decide it.
keys.description: TCU's best players are terrible free throw shooters — Punch (.648), Harding (.646), Edmonds (.650). Ohio State shoots 77.5% from the line (20th nationally). In a tight late-game situation, this disparity could be the difference. Ohio State should attack and foul TCU's bigs late.

keys.title: Can anyone slow down Bruce Thornton?
keys.description: Ohio State's all-time leading scorer is shooting .558 from the floor and .400 from three. He's a 6-2, 215-pound physical guard who's nearly impossible to keep out of the lane. TCU's "no middle" defense is designed to prevent exactly this — but Thornton has seen every defense the Big Ten has thrown at him for four years. This is the matchup within the matchup.

keys.title: Transition game.
keys.description: Both teams want to run. Dixon's Frogs push tempo aggressively, and Diebler's Buckeyes have embraced pace since he took over. This game could be fast and high-scoring. Whichever team controls the tempo — and limits easy transition buckets — gains the edge. Turnover margin is critical.

keys.title: The noon tip.
keys.description: A 12:15 PM ET tip on a Thursday is tricky. Both teams are used to evening games in loud arenas. The early slot at a neutral site tends to produce flat starts and lower energy. The team that comes out locked in from the opening tip — and handles the weird energy of a half-empty arena at lunch — has an advantage.

prediction.summary: This is a classic 8-9 coin flip between two portal-rebuilt teams that caught fire late. TCU won 9 of 10 down the stretch with an interior-first attack; Ohio State has the 16th-best offense nationally driven by Bruce Thornton's historic scoring. The key clash is TCU's interior muscle (Edmonds and Punch) against Ohio State's guard-driven attack (Thornton and Mobley). TCU's "no middle" defensive scheme is designed to stop exactly what Thornton does — drive and create. But Thornton has seen every defense in the Big Ten for four years, and his 20.2 PPG on .558 shooting suggests he'll find a way. The swing factor is free-throw shooting: TCU's bigs are terrible at the line (Punch .648, Harding .646, Edmonds .650), and Ohio State shoots 77.5% as a team. In a tight late-game situation, that gap is decisive.

phases.title: Opening 10 Minutes
phases.description: The noon tip produces a sluggish start for both teams in a half-empty arena. TCU's interior game takes a few possessions to establish against Bynum's 6-8, 240-pound frame. Thornton comes out aggressive, driving into the teeth of TCU's "no middle" defense — he scores 7 early points but turns it over twice. It's 14-12 Ohio State.

phases.title: Mid-First Half to Halftime
phases.description: TCU's frontcourt asserts itself. Edmonds hits a three from the top of the key (he shoots .412 from deep), then Punch blocks a Thornton driving layup. TCU goes on an 8-0 run powered by paint dominance. Mobley answers with a pair of threes — fingers taped together, no hesitation. Halftime: 33-31 TCU in a tight, physical half.

phases.title: Second Half — The Thornton Show
phases.description: This is Bruce Thornton's one shot at March Madness after four years, and he plays like it. He scores 12 second-half points on a mix of pull-up jumpers, drives, and free throws. Ohio State takes a 5-point lead with 8 minutes left. TCU answers with a Punch and-one dunk, and Pierre nails a three to tie it. The crowd finally wakes up.

phases.title: Final 5 Minutes
phases.description: TCU's free-throw shooting becomes the story. Punch misses the front end of a one-and-one. Edmonds goes 1-for-2 at the line. Ohio State capitalizes — Thornton hits two free throws, Mobley sinks two more, and the Buckeyes convert 8-of-10 from the stripe in the final four minutes while TCU goes 3-of-7. Thornton finally gets his March moment.

projectedScore.rationale: Ohio State's free-throw advantage and Bruce Thornton's determination to make his only NCAA Tournament count prove decisive in a tight game. TCU's interior dominance keeps them close, but their inability to convert at the line costs them down the stretch.

players.name: David Punch
players.meta: F • 6-7 • Sophomore • Harker Heights, TX • Big 12 Honorable Mention
players.bio:
A 4-star recruit who chose TCU over NC State and Penn State, Punch has emerged as a potential NBA prospect as a sophomore. He blocks 1.9 shots per game (3rd in the Big 12) and shoots .515 from the floor. In the Big 12 Tournament: 26 points, 7 rebounds, 4 blocks vs. Oklahoma State. His career high: 17 points, 13 rebounds, and 5 blocks against Incarnate Word. The one weakness: .648 from the free throw line.
Read more →

players.name: Xavier Edmonds
players.meta: F • 6-8 • Junior • Long Beach, CA • Big 12 Third Team
players.bio:
The path from Long Beach to Salt Lake Community College to the Big 12 Third Team is one of the best stories in the conference. In his first TCU season, Edmonds had a career-high 26 points on 9-of-10 shooting in an 18-point comeback vs. K-State, then sealed it with two free throws with 6 seconds left. Reeled off five straight double-doubles — the first TCU player to do so since 2017. Then went for 20 and 12 at #10 Texas Tech. Shoots .412 from three — unusual for a 6-8 forward.
Read more →

players.name: Brock Harding
players.meta: G • 6-0 • Junior • Transfer from Iowa • Moline, IL
players.bio: Doesn't score much, but makes everyone else better. The Iowa transfer's 5.7 APG ranks 3rd in the Big 12, and his 3.8 assist-to-turnover ratio reflects a true floor general. He and teammate Jayden Pierre led Moline High to an Illinois state championship as seniors — now they're reunited at TCU running Dixon's up-tempo offense.

players.name: Jayden Pierre
players.meta: G • 6-2 • Senior • Elizabeth, NJ • Transfer from Providence
players.bio: Three years of Big East experience at Providence (97 games, 63 starts) and the best free throw shooter on a team that desperately needs one (.830). His defining moment: a clutch three-pointer that gave TCU a 57-55 lead during the 12-0 closing run that upset #5 Iowa State. When the game is on the line and TCU needs a shot from outside, Pierre is the guy.

players.name: Bruce Thornton
players.meta: G • 6-2, 215 lbs • Senior • Ohio State's All-Time Leading Scorer
players.bio:
The most accomplished player on the floor — and one who has never played in an NCAA Tournament game. Thornton broke Dennis Hopson's 39-year-old scoring record on Senior Day, with Hopson in the building for the ceremony. He's the first four-time captain in Ohio State history. Scoring average every year: 10.6 → 15.7 → 17.7 → 20.2. Shoots .558 from the floor and .400 from three. Only the second Big Ten player ever with 2,000+ points, 500+ assists, and 500+ rebounds.
Read more →

players.name: John Mobley Jr.
players.bio:
They call him "Juni," and he might be the toughest player in this game. After injuring his pinky against USC and undergoing hand surgery, Mobley missed three games, came back with two fingers taped together, and immediately dropped 21 points (5-of-11 from three) in an upset of #8 Purdue. His .418 three-point percentage is one of the best marks in the Big Ten. As a freshman, he hit 77 threes — second in the conference.
Read more →

players.name: Devin Royal
players.meta: F • 6-6 • Junior • Ohio Mr. Basketball • Pickerington, OH
players.bio: The homegrown star. Named Ohio Mr. Basketball at Pickerington Central, where he led his team to back-to-back state title games. Became the 62nd Buckeye to reach 1,000 career points — with a go-ahead three-pointer against Michigan in the Big Ten Tournament. Career high: 31 points and 15 rebounds vs. Valparaiso. A versatile forward who can score inside and out.

players.name: A'mare Bynum
players.bio:
Ohio State "would likely not be in the NCAA Tournament without Bynum." When starter Brandon Noel went down with a foot injury in January, the 4-star freshman from Link Academy seized the role and never looked back. Averaging 12.4 PPG and 6.0 RPG since March 1. Teammates call him "a crazy athlete." A 6-8, 240-pound X-factor playing his best basketball at the best possible time.
Read more →

funFacts.title: Blood-Squirting Mascot (TCU)
funFacts.body: TCU's "Horned Frog" is actually a lizard — the Texas horned lizard. Its most famous defense mechanism: it can shoot blood up to 5 feet from its eyes by rupturing blood vessels in its head. The blood contains a foul-tasting chemical. TCU occasionally wears red accents as a nod to this. More →

funFacts.title: Never Played in March (Ohio State)
funFacts.body: Bruce Thornton is Ohio State's all-time leading scorer with 2,110+ points — and has never played a single NCAA Tournament game. Four years, four captaincies, one record, zero March Madness minutes. This is his one chance.

funFacts.title: Maggie Dixon's Legacy (TCU)
funFacts.body: Jamie Dixon's sister Maggie coached Army women's basketball to the NCAA Tournament in her only season as a head coach. In 2006, they became the first brother-sister pair to take teams to the tournament in the same year. Maggie died weeks later at 28 from a heart arrhythmia. The Maggie Dixon Award honors the top D-I women's coach annually. More →

funFacts.title: 3 Hall of Famers, 1 Team (Ohio State)
funFacts.body: The 1960 national championship team had three future Basketball Hall of Famers on the roster: Jerry Lucas, John Havlicek, and Bob Knight (who entered as a coach). Four total if you count coach Fred Taylor. More →

funFacts.title: Lithuanian Pro (TCU)
funFacts.body: TCU's Liutauras Lelevicius played professionally in Lithuania's Basketball League for Lietkabelis before coming to college. He averaged 17.6 PPG for Lithuania at the 2023 FIBA U20 European Championships. Now he shoots .397 from three and .902 from the line at TCU.

funFacts.title: Fingers Taped Together (Ohio State)
funFacts.body: John Mobley Jr. had hand surgery on his pinky finger in February. He came back with two fingers taped together and immediately scored 21 points (5-of-11 from three) in an upset of #8 Purdue. More →

funFacts.title: 5'3" to the NBA Draft (TCU)
funFacts.body: Jamie Dixon was 5-foot-3 as a freshman point guard at TCU. He grew 11 inches by graduation and led the Southwest Conference in assists as a senior.

funFacts.title: "THE" Ohio State (Ohio State)
funFacts.body: The emphatic "THE" before Ohio State University is one of the most recognizable traditions in American sports — popularized by athletes introducing themselves on national TV. The university uses "The Ohio State University" in its official name. More →

funFacts.title: Nike's CEO (TCU)
funFacts.body: TCU alum Elliott Hill is the current President & CEO of Nike. Other notable TCU grads: NFL Hall of Famer LaDainian Tomlinson, NBA guard Desmond Bane (Memphis Grizzlies), and actor Luke Wilson.

funFacts.title: Germany to Chile to Columbus (Ohio State)
funFacts.body: Ohio State center Christoph Tilly was born in Germany, moved to Chile at age 6, discovered basketball at 9, returned to Germany to play pro for Alba Berlin, then played three years at Santa Clara before transferring. He dropped 28 in the season opener.

funFacts.title: Jesse Owens (Ohio State)
funFacts.body: Ohio State's most famous alum: Jesse Owens, who won four gold medals at the 1936 Berlin Olympics. He also set three world records and tied another in about 45 minutes at the 1935 Big Ten track meet — often called "the greatest 45 minutes in sports."

funFacts.title: The Homecoming Coach (TCU)
funFacts.body: Jamie Dixon played at TCU, led them to the 1987 NCAA Tournament, then came back as head coach in 2016. His pitch: "We're not going to attract recruits based on anything our program has done in the last 30 years. Our future is what we have to sell." Now he's built the most successful stretch in program history.

history.callout: Ohio State brings 11 Final Four appearances and a national championship to this matchup. TCU has never been past the second round. But this isn't about history — it's about two teams that rebuilt through the portal and peaked in March. Ohio State hasn't been to the tournament since 2022. TCU hasn't won a tournament game since 2023. Both are hungry.

subsections.title: Ohio State's 1960 Championship — The Crown Jewel

timeline.text: Three future Hall of Famers: Jerry Lucas (26.3 PPG, 16.0 RPG as a sophomore), John Havlicek, and reserve forward Bob Knight — who entered the Hall as a coach at Indiana.
Ohio State crushed California 75-55 in the title game, capping a 25-3 season. They were the highest-scoring team in college basketball, averaging 90+ points per game.
Lucas and Havlicek led Ohio State to three consecutive championship game appearances (1960-62), but lost to in-state rival Cincinnati in 1961 and 1962.
TCU's first tournament appearance in 20 years. Lost in the first round, but Dixon had proven the program could get there.
Beat Seton Hall 69-42 for TCU's first NCAA win in 35 years — since 1987, when Dixon was a senior player. Lost to #1 Arizona 85-80 in overtime in a heartbreaker.
Beat Arizona State 72-70 in the first round. Lost to #3 Gonzaga 84-81. First time trying to win two tournament games. Mike Miles Jr. scored 24 in the loss.
After a 16-16 season, Dixon overhauled the roster through the portal. Won 9 of their final 10, set a program record with 11 Big 12 wins, and earned a 9-seed.

subsections.title: Head-to-Head History
subsections.content: Ohio State leads the all-time series 4-0, but these two programs haven't played each other since 1969 — 57 years ago. This is essentially a first meeting between two completely different versions of these programs. No current player, coach, or fan has any memory of the last time these teams met.
